The retired West Indies cricketer aims to optimize her team's performance using data-driven strategies. To find the optimal number of practice sessions, we analyze the quadratic equation for the average batting score, \( S = 50 + 5n - 0.2n^2 \). This equation represents a downward-opening parabola, meaning the maximum value occurs at the vertex. The formula for the vertex of a parabola \( ax^2 + bx + c \) is \( n = -b / (2a) \). Here, \( a = -0.2 \) and \( b = 5 \). Substituting these values gives \( n = -5 / (2 \times -0.2) \), which simplifies to \( n = -5 / -0.4 \). The calculation results in \( n = 12.5 \). Since the number of sessions must be a whole number, we test integers 12 and 13. At \( n = 12 \), \( S = 50 + 60 - 28.8 = 81.2 \). At \( n = 13 \), \( S = 50 + 65 - 33.8 = 81.2 \). Both values yield the same maximum score. Thus, the team should aim for 12 or 13 sessions to achieve a maximum average batting score of 81.2.

Next, we calculate the change in win probability when sessions increase from 5 to 10. First, we determine the score for \( n = 5 \). Substituting into the score equation: \( S_5 = 50 + 5(5) - 0.2(25) = 50 + 25 - 5 = 70 \). Using the logistic function \( P = 1 / (1 + e^{-(0.1S - 3)}) \), we find \( P_5 \). The exponent is \( -(0.1 \times 70 - 3) = -(7 - 3) = -4 \). So, \( P_5 = 1 / (1 + e^{-4}) \approx 1 / (1 + 0.0183) \approx 0.9819 \). Now we calculate the score for \( n = 10 \). \( S
